rulings.Case Title: MARTIFER-SILVERADO FUND I, LLC VS. TALESUN SOLAR USA LTD
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Deposition Of Plaintiffs Expert Daniel L. Williams, Or In The Alternative, To Exclude Williams From Testifying At Trial
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, August 30, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT TALESUN SOLAR USA LTD, ZHONGLI NEW ENERGY USA CO., LLC's Motion To Compel Deposition Of Plaintiff's Expert Daniel L. Williams, Or In The Alternative, To Exclude Williams From Testifying At Trial (Part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Adrienne Rogers, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion DENIED without prejudice. Plaintiff designated Williams as an expert in June 2018. Defendants tried, unsuccessfully, to depose Williams. In July 2019, plaintiff withdrew Williams as an expert. Plaintiff stated Williams would "not be providing affirmative opinions" and that plaintiff did "not need an expert to prove damages . . . contained in the contracts themselves." Plaintiff advised defendants it was retaining Williams as a "consulting expert" and reserved the right to call him as a rebuttal expert. A party is entitled to depose a person designated as an expert witness. (Code Civ. Proc., § 2034.410.) A party is entitled to withdraw "a previously designated expert witness, not yet deposed. If that expert continues his . . . relationship with the party as a consultant, the opposing party is barred from communicating with the expert and from retaining him . . . as the opposing party's expert." (County of Los Angeles v. Superior Court (1990) 222 Cal.App.3d 647, 657-658.) " 'Nevertheless the initial status of the expert, as consultant and possible witness, changes its character at that point in the suit when it has become known he will actually testify as a witness. When it becomes reasonably certain an expert will give his professional opinion as a witness on a material matter in dispute, then his opinion has become a factor in the cause. At that point the expert has ceased to be merely a consultant and has become a counter in the litigation, one to be evaluated along with others. Such evaluation properly includes appropriate pretrial discovery.' " (DeLuca v. State Fish Co., Inc. (2013) 217 Cal.App.4th 671, 689-690.) (Continued to Part 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARTIFER-SILVERADO FUND I, LLC VS. TALESUN SOLAR USA LTD
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Talesun Solar Usa Ltd, Zhongli New Energy Usa Co., Llc'S Motion To Compel Deposition Of Plaintiff'S Expert Daniel L. Williams, Or In The Alternative, To Exclude Williams From Testifying At Trial (Part 2 Of 2 - For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, August 30, 2019, Line 1, Defendant Talesun Solar Usa Ltd, Zhongli New Energy Usa Co., Llc'S Motion To Compel Deposition Of Plaintiff'S Expert Daniel L. Williams, Or In The Alternative, To Exclude Williams From Testifying At Trial (Part 2 Of 2 - For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only) Defendants repeatedly claim it is reasonably certain Williams will testify as an expert, but repetition does not make it so. Defendants' reliance on deposition testimony given by PMQ Hans Isern does not establish it is reasonably certain Williams will testify as an expert, particularly because Isern has offered a declaration averring his communications with Williams occurred in the context of a different case pending between the parties. (See also Burtt Decl. 2-4.) A damages assessment Williams apparently prepared in the other case pending between the parties does not establish Williams formulated opinions in this case, or that it is reasonably certain Williams will give expert testimony in this case. Defendants have not established they will be prejudiced if they cannot depose Williams before trial. This court expresses no opinion on whether plaintiff may be entitled to call Williams as a rebuttal expert or for purposes of impeachment or whether exclusion of Williams's trial testimony is appropriate. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to adrienneleight@hotmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of tentative ruling) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: THERESA M. MOORE ET AL VS. MARY T. MOORE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Mtn For Evidentiary Issue Sanctions Or In The Alt For Terminating Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, August 30, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF THERESA MOORE, SCOTT GUSTAFSON Notice Of Motion And Mtn For Evidentiary Issue Sanctions Or In The Alt For Terminating Sanctions (Part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Adrienne Rogers, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is DENIED without prejudice. A court may impose issue, evidence, or terminating sanctions for a party's failure to obey an order compelling discovery. (Code Civ. Proc., §§ 2023.030(b)-(d).) A motion for issue or evidentiary sanctions must be supported by a separate statement. (Cal. Rules of Court, rule 3.1345(a)(7).) Plaintiffs' motion was not supported by a separate statement, which is grounds to deny the motion. (Mills v. U.S. Bank (2008) 166 Cal.App.4th 871, 893.) A separate statement, which describes the complaint, the cross-complaint, and the discovery requests, and identifies the specific issues that should be deemed established, and/or the specific evidence that should be excluded, will assist the court in determining whether issue and evidentiary sanctions are warranted. (Cal. Rules of Court, rule 3.1345(c)(4), (c)(6).) To the extent a separate statement is not required, the court concludes the evidence is insufficient to support issue or evidentiary sanctions. The evidence before the court is (1) defendant has not complied with the June 14, 2019 order compelling her to provide discovery responses and pay monetary sanctions; and (2) defendant sent an email on May 3, 2019 stating she would not respond to the discovery requests. The email, sent before court ordered defendant to respond to the discovery, does not tend to establish defendant's failure to comply with the court order was willful. (Biles v. Exxon Mobil Corp. (2004) 124 Cal.App.4th 1315, 1327.) "[S]anctions may not be imposed solely to punish the offending party," and "the sanction chosen should not provide a windfall to the other party, by putting the prevailing party in a better position than if he or she had obtained the discovery sought and it had been favorable." (Rutledge v. Hewlett Packard Co. (2015) 238 Cal.App.4th 1164, 1193.) Based on the evidence before the court, imposing issue or evidentiary sanctions would provide a windfall to plaintiffs. (Continued to Part 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: THERESA M. MOORE ET AL VS. MARY T. MOORE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Theresa Moore, Scott Gustafson'S Motion For Evidentiary Issue Sanctions Or In The Alt For Terminating Sanctions (Part 2 Of 2 - For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, August 30, 2019, Line 2, Plaintiffs Theresa Moore, Scott Gustafson's Motion For Evidentiary Issue Sanctions Or In The Alt For Terminating Sanctions (Part 2 Of 2 - For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only). The request for terminating sanctions is also denied without prejudice. Plaintiffs have not established a persistent and serious misuse of the discovery process, or that less severe sanctions would be ineffective. (See Doppes v. Bentley Motors, Inc. (2009) 174 Cal.App.4th 967, 992-994; Lang v. Hochman (2000) 77 Cal.App.4th 1225, 1246.) rulings.Case Title: CAVALRY SPV I, LLC VS. ELIZABETH AGUERO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order: Truth Of Matters Specified In Requests For Admissions Be Deemed Admitted
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, August 30, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF CAVALRY SPV I, LLC's Motion For Order: Truth Of Matters Specified In Requests For Admissions Be Deemed Admitted. Pro Tem Judge Adrienne Rogers, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion that matters specified in requests for admission be deemed admitted. (Code Civ. Proc., § 2033.280(b), statutory references are to the Code of Civil Procedure.) The unopposed motion is GRANTED. Plaintiff served Requests for Admission, Set One (RFAs) by mail on April 29, 2019. Defendant's responses were due on June 3, 2019. Defendant did not respond to the RFAs. The court must order the RFAs deemed admitted unless plaintiff serves, before the hearing on the motion, a proposed response in substantial compliance with section 2033.220. (St. Mary v. Superior Court (2014) 233 Cal.App.4th 762, 774 (St. Mary); § 2033.280(c).) Before the hearing, plaintiff did not serve a proposed response. The RFAs are deemed admitted against defendant. "Matters that are . . . deemed admitted through [request for admissions] discovery devices are conclusively established in the litigation and are not subject to being contested through contradictory evidence." (St. Mary, at p. 775.) The court must impose monetary sanctions upon the party and/or the attorney for the failure to serve a timely response to the RFAs. (§ 2033.280(c); St. Mary, supra, 223 Cal.App.4th at p. 776.) Here, plaintiff does not seek monetary sanctions and offers no evidence of the costs or attorney fees incurred to bring the motion. Under the circumstances, the court declines to award monetary sanctions. (See § 2023.040.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to adrienneleight@hotmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
